coredata.dc:title: A new enhanced chemiluminescence reaction based on polymer dots for the determination of metronidazole
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Haghighi Shishavan, Yalda
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots (PDs) with non-conjugated functional groups are attracting nanomaterials due to their ease of synthesis, the biocompatibility of precursors, and low toxicity. In this work, PDs with non-conjugated groups were synthesized with a simple and straightforward method by Schiff base reaction. Then their possible application in the chemiluminescence (CL) reactions was explored. Results were shown that PDs increased the CL intensity of the NaIO4-fluorescein system about 15 times. Regarding the CL mechanism, we proved that the emitting species is fluorescein, which can be excited by the energy transfer from the excited-state PDs. It was observed that CL emission is promoted by the interaction of metronidazole (MND) with the PDs. Therefore, we designed a novel and sensitive assay for MND based on its enhancing effect on NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs CL system. The introduced assay showed a linear response in the range of 5.0–300 nM with a detection limit of 1.5 nM. The method was used for the determination of MND in spiked plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Nanomaterials
Antibiotics
Fluorescein
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of PDs on the NaIO4-fluorescein CL
Application of the CL system for metronidazole assay
Determination of MND in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectrum of HQ, EDA, and PDs (a), Fluorescence emission spectra of PDs at different excitation wavelengths (b), excitation (λem = 510 nm) and emission (λex = 410 nm) spectra of PDs (c), FT-IR spectrum of HQ (A) and PDs (B) (d), TEM image of PDs (inset shows the size distribution obtained by DLS method) (e), EDS spectrum of PDs (f).
Kinetic profile of NaIO4-fluorescein (A), NaIO4-PDs (B), NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs (C). Condition: NaIO4 (1.5 mM), fluorescein (0.25 mM), and PDs (75 µL).

CL spectra of NaIO4-flourescein CL reaction in the alkaline medium in the absence of PDs; NaIO4 (1 mM) in one line and fluorescein (0.25 mM) in other line (A) and in the presence of PDs; NaIO4 (1 mM) in one line and fluorescein (0.25 mM) and PDs solution in another line (B), fluorescence spectrum of fluorescein (inset C).
Calibration graph for MND (a), and corresponding CL time profiles (b) under optimal conditions.
The effect of diverse radical scavengers on the CL reaction of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system.
Comparison of methods for the determination of MND.
Results for the determination of MND in real samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A new enhanced chemiluminescence reaction based on polymer dots for the determination of metronidazole

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dots (PDs) with non-conjugated functional groups are attracting nanomaterials due to their ease of synthesis, the biocompatibility of precursors, and low toxicity. In this work, PDs with non-conjugated groups were synthesized with a simple and straightforward method by Schiff base reaction. Then their possible application in the chemiluminescence (CL) reactions was explored. Results were shown that PDs increased the CL intensity of the NaIO4-fluorescein system about 15 times. Regarding the CL mechanism, we proved that the emitting species is fluorescein, which can be excited by the energy transfer from the excited-state PDs. It was observed that CL emission is promoted by the interaction of metronidazole (MND) with the PDs. Therefore, we designed a novel and sensitive assay for MND based on its enhancing effect on NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs CL system. The introduced assay showed a linear response in the range of 5.0–300 nM with a detection limit of 1.5 nM. The method was used for the determination of MND in spiked plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have been widely used as both prescription and nonprescription drugs in the treatment of humans and domestic animals, and also as promoters of aquatic growth [1,2]. Metronidazole (MND) is a derivative of nitroimidazoles that has antibacterial and antiprotozoal properties [3,4]. Long-term use of this drug may have side effects such as carcinogenesis, seizures, central nervous system disorders, cardiovascular problems, and anemia [5,6]. Thus, it is essential to develop a sensitive, rapid, facile, and affordable method for the MND assay in water, soil, and human plasma. Some of the analytical techniques that have been introduced for this purpose over the past decade include fluorescence [7], electrochemistry [8], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], ion mobility spectrometry [10], colorimetric assay [11] and chemiluminescence (CL) [12].

A CL process involves the redox reaction between reagents to form an excited-state species, which eventually emits the electromagnetic radiation. CL is a powerful analytical method due to its high sensitivity, wide linear range, low detection limit, affordable instruments, and fast response [13–19].

In recent years, nanosized materials have widely been used for optical sensing and detection [20,21]. Carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs) are a group of these nanosized materials that have broad applications due to their optical, electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties [22–24]. Polymer dots (PDs) as a new kind of CNMs have attracted much attention in recent years since they have inherent properties such as photostability, good biocompatibility, affordable production, and good photophysical properties [25]. Due to these features, they have found many applications in the field of chemical sensors and bioanalysis [26,27]. PDs are classified into two main groups: conjugated polymer dots (CPDs) and non-conjugated polymer dots (NCPDs). CPDs are formed by the assembly of conjugated polymers and have π conjugated bonds [28]. They have disadvantages such as severe toxicity, low water solubility, complicated production process, and the use of environmentally damaging solvents [29]. NCPDs are constructed from non-conjugated polymers or small molecules containing functional groups of hydroxyl, carbonyl, or amine through polymerization, entanglement, crosslinking, carbonization, and dehydration processes [30]. NCPDs eliminate the disadvantages of CPDs and have low toxicity, water-solubility, fast and facile synthesis, and biocompatible precursors [31].

In the present work, we have synthesized green fluorescent NCPDs via a single-step, environmentally friendly method by self-assembly and polymerization. Then we investigated the performance of these PDs in the NaIO4-fluorescein CL system. It was found that PDs considerably increased the CL signal of this reaction. Furthermore, it was observed that low amounts of MND increased the CL intensity of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system, so a very sensitive CL probe was designed for MND.

The structure and morphology of the synthesized PDs were determined by TEM image (Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany; www.zeiss.com), DLS (Nanotrac Wave, Microtrac, www.microtrac.com), EDS spectroscopy (MIRA III, France; www.tescan.com), and FT-IR spectrometry (Bruker Co., Germany; www.bruker.com). The absorption spectrum was obtained with a Cary-100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). CL analyses were performed with a LUMAT LB 9507 instrument (Berthold, Germany; www.berthold.com), which has automatic injectors. Fluorescence and CL spectra were obtained by the Jasco FP-8300 spectrofluorimeter (Japan; www.jascoinc.com).

Hydroquinone (HQ, C6H6O2), ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H8N2), NaIO4, fluorescein (C20H12O5) and NaOH were acquired from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Metronidazole (C6H9N3O3, 99%) was obtained from Tehran Pars Company (Iran) and its stock solution (0.01 M) was prepared in deionized water. Sodium periodate solution with a concentration of 0.01 M was prepared daily. The 0.01 M solution of fluorescein was prepared by dissolving a certain amount of this substance in 1.0 M NaOH and it was reached to 100 mL with deionized water and kept at 4 °C.

PDs were synthesized by the following method [32]: 1.0 mL of 1.0 M EDA solution was added to 20 mL of 0.1 M HQ solution and mixed for 10 min in an ultrasonic bath. Afterward, the mixture was heated in a water bath at 50 °C for 2 h. The product was centrifuged and purified by a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 2000 Da) for 2 h. The final product was used for the next steps.

CL tests were performed on a 3-mL tube. Briefly, 25 μL of fluorescein (0.01 M) and 75 μL of PDs were poured into the cell. Then the appropriate volumes of sample or standard MND solution were added to the cell, and the final volume reached 850 μL with deionized water. In the next step, 150 μL of NaIO4 (0.01 M) was injected in order to trigger the CL reaction, and the CL intensity was recorded.

500 μL of acetonitrile was added to 500 μL of a plasma sample and centrifuged for 45 min. The upper transparent solution was collected and reached to 2.5 mL with deionized water. Then 250 μL of this solution was used for analysis. To validate the proposed method, a certain amount of analyte was spiked into the plasma samples before the pretreatment steps.

HQ has two hydroxyl groups bonded to a benzene ring in a para position and could be oxidized to parabenzoquinone in the alkaline solution. The resulted benzoquinone is rapidly polymerized by covalent bonding and aggregation [33]. When EDA, as a relatively strong basic amine, was added to HQ, Schiff base reaction occurs between ketones groups of p-benzoquinone and amino groups of EDA. The product is immediately polymerized and self-assembled to form a product containing CN bonds [34]. The formation of the PDs is shown in scheme 1. (Electronic Supplementary Material, ESM). The optical properties of the PDs were studied by fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 1 a shows the absorption spectrum of HQ, EDA, and PDs. In the HQ spectrum two absorption peaks at about 220 and 290 nm are seen, which are related to the π–π* transitions of the benzene ring, redshifted due to the presence of –OH auxochrome. The EDA spectrum has a peak around 220 nm which is related to the n–σ* transition of C-N bond. In the UV–Vis spectrum of PDs, a new peak is seen at around 410 nm, which is related to the n-π* transition of the CN bond. Fig. 1b shows the fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths. As seen in Fig. 1b, by increasing the excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength remains unchanged (The optimum values for λex and λem were 410 and 510 nm, respectively). The PD solution emits green fluorescence under the UV light (inset of Fig. 1c). The quantum yield (QY) of the PDs was obtained 34.4% using fluorescein dissolved in 1 M NaOH as a reference (QY = 0.95).

In Fig. 1d, a comparison has been made between the FT-IR spectrum of HQ and PDs. As can be seen in curve B, the peak intensity of the OH stretching vibration at 3351 cm−1 was drastically reduced and a new peak appeared in 1633 cm−1, which is related to the CN bond and is the evidence of PDs formation by Schiff base reaction [32]. As can be seen in the TEM image (Fig. 1e), the synthesized PDs have diameters of 10 ± 4 nm. The inset in Fig. 1e represents the particle size distribution of the PDs obtained by DLS technique. The particle size of the PDs is mainly distributed in the range of 6–14 nm and the average size is 11 nm. Finally, the EDS spectrum confirmed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in the PDs structure (Fig. 1f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of PDs on the NaIO4-fluorescein CL
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the synthesized PDs on the sodium periodate-fluorescein CL system in the alkaline medium was studied. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the NaIO4-fluorescein system does not show significant CL, but in the presence of PDs the CL signal is affected (Fig. 2C) and intensity increases about 15-fold. The CL reaction was also examined by using raw materials (HQ, EDA). These components did not affect the NaIO4-fluorescein CL system, and it can be concluded that the increase in the CL intensity was related to the PDs. We also examined the direct NaIO4-PDs reaction, but as seen in Fig. 2B, the CL intensity was again very low.

In the next step, the CL mechanism of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system was studied. Fig. 3 shows the CL spectrum of this system, recorded by a spectrofluorimeter. A distinct emission maximum at about 520 nm was obtained in the presence of PDs. This corresponds to the fluorescence spectrum of fluorescein (inset C in Fig. 3). In the absence of PDs, since the CL intensity was very low, no observable peak appeared in the CL spectrum. The possible mechanism of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system can be explained as follows: In this system, reactive oxygen species (ROS) (such as OH
and O2
− radicals) are generated from the reaction of dissolved O2 in alkaline solution with periodate [14]. The O2
− can inject electrons into the PDs to generate PDs −. On the other hand, oxidant (NaIO4) and OH
inject holes into the PDs and PDs + are produced [35]. Then the electron-hole annihilation between PD + and PD − occurs and, as a result, excited-state PDs (PDs*) are produced. As Fig. S1a (ESM) shows, the fluorescence emission of PDs is in a wavelength region of 450 to 650 nm (curve A), which overlaps with the absorption spectrum of fluorescein (in the wavelength range of 420 to 540 nm, curve B). Therefore energy transfer can occur from PDs* to fluorescein and the excited fluorescein molecules are produced and emit at about 520 nm [36,37]. To prove the generation of ROS, the effect of some radical scavengers was investigated. For this purpose radical scavengers including DMSO, thiourea, and sodium azide were added into CL system to investigate the production of radical OH
, O2
− and 1O2*, respectively. As shown in Table 1 , the CL intensity in the presence of these radical scavengers was restrained. According to these results, ROS play a crucial role in the CL reaction.

It should be noted that the fluorescence emission maximum of PDs is around 510 nm, which is quite close to the CL maximum wavelength (520 nm). So it may be possible that we consider PDs* as CL emitters. According to Fig. 2B, the CL intensity of the NaIO4-PDs system is very low, while in the presence of fluorescein the intensity increases significantly. To explain this, we should consider an energy transfer from excited-state fluorescein to PDs. But according to Fig. S1b (ESM), there is no overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of fluorescein, and the absorption spectrum of PDs. Therefore, such energy transfer would not be possible, and this mechanism cannot be proved.

To achieve the best performance of the CL system, the impact of some factors such as NaIO4, fluorescein, NaOH concentration, and amount of PDs on the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs CL reaction were investigated and the CL signal in the presence (I2) and absence (I1) of PDs was recorded, and I = I2/I1 was calculated. The results are summarized in Figs. (S2–S5) (ESM).

To study the effect of NaIO4 concentration, solutions with different concentrations of NaIO4 were prepared over the range of 0.25–2.5 mM. The maximum CL response was obtained for 1.5 mM NaIO4 (Fig. S2, ESM). At lower concentrations, the number of ROS and thus the CL intensity was low.

The effect of fluorescein concentration on the CL was examined in the range of 0.1–1.0 mM. The CL response increased by increasing fluorescein concentration up to 0.25 mM (Fig. S3, ESM). The influence of NaOH concentration in fluorescein solution was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S4 (ESM), 1.0 mM NaOH produce the best result. So 0.25 mM fluorescein in 1.0 mM NaOH was chosen as the optimal solution.

Finally, the effect of amount of PDs on the CL response over the range of 25–150 μL was studied. According to the results (Fig. S5, ESM), the intensity increased by increasing PDs up to 75 μL. So this amount was selected for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the CL system for metronidazole assay
ce:section.ce:para: We observed that the CL intensity of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system increases upon the addition of trace amounts of MND. To examine the possible mechanism of this effect we performed several experiments. Firstly, the interaction of MND with NaIO4 was tested by using the CL and absorption spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. S6a (ESM), no CL was obtained for the NaIO4-MND system. The absorption spectrum of MND also did not change upon addition of NaIO4 (Fig. S7, ESM). Therefore, there is no interaction between MND and NaIO4. Second, the effect of MND on the NaIO4-fluorescein system was examined. As seen in Fig. S6b and S6c (ESM), MND had no effect on this reaction. So we can conclude that there is no interaction between fluorescein and MND. On the other hand, when MND was added to NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system, a remarkable increase occurred in the CL (Fig. S6d and S6e). According to the fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. S8, (ESM), MND had no direct enhancing effect on the fluorescence of PDs. This means that MND did not change the QY of PD emission. Taking all these experiments into consideration, the following explanation can be proposed for the effect of MND: since MND has hydroxyl and nitro groups, these groups may combine with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of PDs by hydrogen bonding. This phenomenon will improve the energy transfer from PDs to fluorescein and significantly increase the CL signal [36].

Due to the sensitivity of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system to MND, the determination of this analyte can be performed. As shown in Fig. 4 , in the optimal conditions, a linear calibration was obtained for MND from 5.0 to 300 nM with a detection limit of 1.5 nM (S/N = 3). The calibration equation was ΔI = 9.7808C + 2959.9, R2 = 0.9992, where ΔI = I2−I1 (I2 is CL intensity in the presence MND in nM and I1 is the intensity in the absence of MND). The relative standard deviation (RSD %) was 2.2% for five replicate assays of 50 nM MND. Table 2 gives a comparison of this method for the determination of MND with some other methods. As can be seen, this probe has a lower detection limit than other methods.

The effect of some antibiotics and other species on the CL signal of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system was assessed in the presence of MND. The results of this scrutiny are shown in Table S1 (ESM). The tolerance limit of all species was evaluated in a relative error of 5%. According to these results, the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system had a suitable selectivity for the determination of MND.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MND in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system was employed for the determination of MND in plasma samples. At this step, two different samples of human plasma were used for analysis. The results of these analyses are reported in Table 3 . The recovery rates for MND were within the 99 to 103% range and the Student t-test showed no significant differences between added and found contents. These results affirmed that the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system could be used for MND determination in human plasma.

PDs, synthesized by a facile method, enhance the CL intensity of the NaIO4-fluorescein system. The CL stems from the excited-state fluorescein. Due to the overlap of the emission spectrum of PDs with the absorption spectrum of fluorescein molecules, the process of energy transfer from the excited PDs to the fluorescein occurs and the excited-state fluorescein is produced. The CL signal of the NaIO4-fluorescein-PDs system is amplified in the presence of MND, which can be attributed to the interaction of hydroxyl and nitro groups of MND with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of PDs by hydrogen bonding and the facilitation of the energy transfer process. This phenomenon formed the basis for developing an assay for MND determination in human plasma samples.
Yalda Haghighi Shishavan: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119992.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
